---
title: "Week 2 using Quarto and Xaringan"
subtitle: "Building reproducible and accessible outputs"
author: "Matt Koller"
institute: "CASA0023 – Remote Sensing"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "styles.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

class: inverse, center, middle

# Why do these tools matter?

---

# Why this week matters

This week focused less on remote sensing theory and more on how analytical work can be structured, communicated, and shared effectively.

The main tools introduced were Quarto for creating reproducible portfolios and reports and Xaringan for building presentations using R Markdown.

Together these tools support a workflow where analysis and communication are closely linked.

<div style="margin-top: 25px; padding: 12px 16px; border-left: 5px solid #4db6ac; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.08);">
A strong workflow does not just help you analyse data.
It helps you explain it clearly and update it efficiently.
</div>

---

# Quarto and Xaringan

.pull-left[

## Quarto

Quarto is useful for structured written outputs.

It brings together text code figures and interpretation in one place.

This makes it useful for:

- learning diaries
- technical reports
- documentation
- portfolios

]

.pull-right[

## Xaringan

Xaringan is useful for presentations built from Markdown.

It allows presentations to be:

- version controlled
- updated quickly
- hosted online
- embedded into other outputs

]

---

# Reproducibility

A key theme this week was reproducibility.

This means outputs can be regenerated consistently.

Methods are clearly documented.

Updates can be made without rebuilding everything manually.

Analysis is easier to review explain and maintain.

This is especially useful when work needs to be shared or revisited over time.

<div style="margin-top: 20px; text-align: center; font-size: 1.2em; font-weight: 600; color: #3aa6b9;">
Reproducibility makes analysis easier to trust.
</div>

---

background-image: url(https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1516321318423-f06f85e504b3?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1600&q=80)
background-size: cover
background-position: center
class: inverse, bottom

# Communication matters

### Good analysis has limited value if people cannot understand or use it

---

# Workflow benefits

Using Quarto and Xaringan together creates a more joined up workflow.

The same project can include written reflections.

It can include analytical outputs.

It can include figures and maps.

It can include presentations for wider audiences.

This reduces duplication and helps keep outputs more consistent.

<div style="margin-top: 25px; display: flex; gap: 14px;">
  <div style="flex: 1; padding: 14px; border-radius: 10px; background: #e3f2fd; color: #111;">
    <strong>Before</strong><br/><br/>
    Separate notes<br/>
    Separate slides<br/>
    Manual copying and updating
  </div>
  <div style="flex: 1; padding: 14px; border-radius: 10px; background: #e8f5e9; color: #111;">
    <strong>After</strong><br/><br/>
    One structured workflow<br/>
    Easier updates<br/>
    Better consistency
  </div>
</div>

---

# Professional relevance

.pull-left[

These tools have clear value beyond university work.

In a professional setting reproducible outputs can support:

- Sharing findings across teams.
- Reducing manual editing.
- Keeping reports up to date.
- Improving transparency in analysis.

]

.pull-right[

This is particularly useful when technical work needs to be communicated to non technical audiences.

That makes these tools relevant not only for coursework but also for real world analytical practice.

<div style="margin-top: 18px; padding: 12px 14px; border-radius: 10px; background: #fff3e0; color: #111;">
Clear presentation is not separate from analysis.
It is part of making analysis useful.
</div>

]

---

# My experience this week

I found Quarto relatively intuitive once the project structure was in place. I have been able to make several websites using the book format, both at work and for Uni.

Xaringan was more challenging at first because it required a different way of thinking compared to traditional presentation tools.

However it also showed clear advantages, like:

It is more flexible than PowerPoint.

It is easier to update once built.

It is better integrated with reproducible workflows.

<div style="margin-top: 20px; padding: 12px 16px; border-left: 5px solid #ffb74d; background: #fff8e1; color: #111;">
My main takeaway was that these tools feel harder at the start but more efficient once the structure is working.
</div>

---

class: inverse, center, middle

# Practical challenges

---

# Challenges

There are also practical limitations.

These include:

- Learning the syntax and structure.

- Understanding how files link together.

- Rendering issues.

- Embedding outputs correctly.

- Hosting content through GitHub Pages

- The setup can take time but the long term benefits make it worthwhile.

<div style="margin-top: 22px; text-align: center; font-size: 1.1em; color: #ef5350; font-weight: 600;">
There is a learning curve, but it pays off once the workflow is established.
</div>

---

# Key takeaway

This week reinforced that good analytical work is not just about producing results.

It is also about:

- Structuring work clearly.
- Making outputs reproducible.
- Communicating effectively.
- Building workflows that can be maintained over time.

These are important skills for both academic and professional practice.

---

background-image: url(https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1451187580459-43490279c0fa?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1600&q=80)
background-size: cover
background-position: center
class: center, middle, inverse

# Thank you

## Week 2 presentation created with xaringan

### Reproducible work is easier to share maintain and trust
